msgs.text:
Turn 24 — coefficient layer reframed as a ghost/Bockstein problem I re-read the three current attachments only. The original target remains the over-G isomorphism question for the two quotients ΓS,ΓH, with F=P⋊G, P the free G-operator pro-2 group on x0,x1, and the requested condition πH∘Φ=πS.

Turn 23 left the right gap: Turn 21’s compactness framework is sound only after truncating both Magnus degree and Λ=Z2 [[G]]-coefficients, but nonemptiness of the finite coefficient-level solution sets SN,J is still not proved. Turn 23 also correctly rejected the square-zero formal-smoothness route, because the residual even-layer mechanism uses nonlinear restricted squares W↦W[2], which disappear in a square-zero tangent calculation.

The new point of this turn is:the coefficient problem should be tested by ghost pairs, not by tangent maps.This is a structural reduction of the coefficient gap, not yet a final proof of ΓS≅GΓH.Let

R:=EfΛ,t:=s−1,so locally

R≃Z2 [[t]],m=(2,t).

Turn 23 singled out the first two length-two coefficient quotientsA2=R/(4,t),At=R/(2,t2),corresponding to the two generators 2 and t=s−1 of m.

After the Turn 1 H1 -change, the dangerous Ef-linear row is(s2+1, s−1).

In terms of t=s−1,s2+1=(1+t)2+1=2+2t+t2,so the two first coefficient directions enter as(s2+1, s−1)≡{ (2,0)(0,t)over A2,over At.This is the key observation. The coefficient directions are not arbitrary new obstructions; they are the two ghost components of the same Nielsen correction mechanism.

1. The 2-direction is a Witt/Bockstein ghost, not a tangent directionOverA2=Z2 [[t]]/(4,t)≅Z/4,an actual group correction with initial primitive form W has Magnus expansion K=1+W+⋯.

Its square has the beginningK2=1+2W+W[2]+⋯.So the 2-coefficient tail 2W and the degree-doubled square tail W[2]are coupled. A square-zero tangent calculation only sees the 2W-part and misses W[2], which is exactly why the Turn 22 square-zero Hensel lemma was too strong.

Thus the right 2-Bockstein correction map is not

W⟼2W and not separately

W⟼W[2],but the ghost-pair mapG2 (W)=(2W, W[2]).This is the coefficient analogue of the residual transgression already used in Turns 15–18. In the residual problem, Turn 18 provedCm=Lm (m≥5),so every residual square quotient L2m/im∂2m−1≅Lm[2]is hit by W↦W[2].The coefficient refinement says: at the 2-ghost level, one must kill the pair (2W,W[2]) together. That is precisely what actual restricted representatives do, while naked tangent vectors do not.

So the A2 test does not introduce a new independent square obstruction. It introduces a compatibility condition:the degree-2m square class must be the square of the same W whose lower ghost is 2W.Any obstruction to the 2-coefficient lift must therefore be a failure of this ghost compatibility, not a new class in Lm[2]itself.

2. The t=s−1-direction is derivational, not Frobenius-squareOverAt=R/(2,t2),we have characteristic 2, t2=0, and s=1+t,s2=1.

The linear row becomes(s2+1, s−1)=(0,t).So the first t-coefficient direction is supplied directly by the s−1 coefficient of the second correction variable. Unlike the 2-direction, this is not a restricted-square phenomenon. It is an infinitesimal s-derivation.Concretely, if a correction has primitive initial form V, then the t-ghost begins asGt (V)=tV together with ordinary commutator/derivation terms in the next Magnus layer. Those next-layer terms lie in the same ordinary boundary complex∂n (U,V)=[A,U]+[B,V],whose odd layers were already formal boundaries and whose even residual cokernels were handled by the Cm=Lm induction. The history records exactly this mechanism: odd layers are ordinary boundaries, while even residual square quotients are killed by root transgressions.Thus the t-direction does not create a new Frobenius-square quotient. It only creates a derivational ghost that is absorbed by the same lower-plus-high torsor, provided the ordinary continuation condition Cm=Lm is used rather than a fixed-gauge tail.

3. Unified coefficient formulation The correct finite coefficient object is therefore not a square-zero lifting mapSN (A)→SN (A/a).It is a ghost-compatible nonemptiness problem over each finite quotient A of R. For each coefficient monomial μ∈mr/mr+1, write μ as a product of 2’s and t’s. Then peel off one generator:μ={2μ′tμ′ in the 2-Bockstein case,in the t-derivation case.

The two elementary ghost moves are:G2 (W)=(2μ′ W, μ′2W[2]),andGt (V)=tμ′ V+ordinary derivation boundary.The first is the Witt/Frobenius ghost. The second is the s-derivation ghost.

This gives a much better induction target:Every coefficient obstruction is killed if its 2- and t-ghost components satisfy the natural group-like compatibility.

The residual theorem Cm=Lm supplies the root W for the Frobenius component, and the ordinary boundary part supplies the derivational t-component. The prior residual induction is therefore exactly the right base theorem, but it must be used through this ghost-pair formalism, not through square-zero smoothness.

4. What this does and does not close This turn does not yet prove the final coefficient nonemptiness lemma.It does rule out the wrong next move. Testing A2 and At as two unrelated finite computations would miss the structure. The A2 direction is coupled to W[2]throughK2=1+2W+W[2]+⋯,while the At direction is coupled to the s-derivation through s=1+t. These are two faces of the same coefficient ghost complex.So the remaining finite coefficient gap has sharpened to one uniform statement:Ghost compatibility lemma.

For every finite quotient A of R=Z2 [[t]], every finite Magnus degree N, and every coefficient-level error arising from the relator equation, the error’s 2- and t-ghost components lie in the image of the lower-plus-high correction torsor generated by

W↦(2W,W[2])and V↦tV+ordinary s-derivation boundary.If this lemma is proved, then the Turn 21 compactness framework closes: the finite solution sets SN,J are nonempty, the coefficient-and-degree inverse system has a compatible limit, and the limit over-G automorphism descends to an isomorphism ΓS≅GΓH. The compactness mechanism and quotient conclusion are already recorded in the history once finite coefficient nonemptiness is available.

So the state is now cleaner:residual obstruction tower closed+coefficient compactness framework correct+coefficient ghost compatibility still to prove.I would still not state the final theorem as proved. But the remaining gap is no longer “test arbitrary Artinian coefficient quotients.” It is the much narrower ghost-compatibility lemma for the two generators 2 and t=s−1, with higher m-adic layers obtained by iterating those two moves.

ESTABLISHED: The coefficient lift should be formulated as a ghost/Bockstein nonemptiness problem, not as square-zero formal smoothness. The first coefficient directions are controlled by(s2+1,s−1)=(2+2t+t2,t),so A2 sees the Witt ghost (2W,W[2]), while At sees the s-derivation ghost tV+ ordinary boundary. This is a uniform structural reduction of the coefficient gap.

GAP: The ghost compatibility lemma is not yet proved: one must still show that every actual finite coefficient error arising from the relator equation satisfies the required 2/t-ghost compatibility and is therefore killed by the lower-plus-high torsor.

NEXT: Prove the ghost compatibility lemma directly from group-likeness of the Magnus error and the identities K2=1+2W+W[2]+⋯, s=1+t. Then induct on m=(2,t)-adic order to obtain SN,J=∅ for every coefficient-and-degree truncation.[TURN24-COEFFICIENT-GHOST-COMPLEX]
